judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports independently identify the same root cause, fix, and reset-gap mechanism with comparable precision and file-level specificity; Report 2 adds minor extra findings (auth gap, connection-close hygiene) but both fully satisfy all three expectations equally well.

report_1.evidence: cedarline/.env and .env.example both set: DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar ... Table owners bypass RLS by default unless the table also has FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY set — and 0001_init.sql never sets FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY on either table.

report_2.evidence: This is the only DATABASE_URL the application ever sees ... PostgreSQL's row-level security is, by design, never applied to a table's owner unless the table is additionally marked FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY — and 0001_init.sql only issues ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY, never FORCE.

report_1.evidence: Stop the app from ever connecting as cedar_owner. Change cedarline/.env / .env.example and backend/app/db.py so the running application ... connects only via cedar_app's credentials. Concretely: rename the ambiguous single DATABASE_URL into two explicit variables, e.g. APP_DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar (consumed by backend/app/db.py) and ADMIN_DATABASE_URL / MIGRATIONS_DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar (consumed only by scripts/migrate.sh and the one-time bootstrap).

report_2.evidence: change DATABASE_URL in cedarline/.env (and .env.example) so the running application connects as cedar_app, not cedar_owner ... Separate roles by responsibility, strictly. cedar_owner should be used only by migration/DDL tooling ... cedar_app should be the only credential the API service itself ever uses, and it should stay non-owner/non-superuser so RLS keeps applying to it.

report_1.evidence: db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql is explicitly a manual, one-time step ... 'Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup.' ... reset-local-db.sh ... down -v deletes the named volume cedar_pgdata ... migrate.sh only loops over db/migrations/*.sql ... it never touches db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql ... after reset-local-db.sh runs, the fresh cluster has cedar_owner (recreated by Postgres/POSTGRES_USER) and the schema ..., but no cedar_app role at all.

report_2.evidence: cedar_app is created by exactly one file, cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql, whose own header comment says: 'One-time bootstrap: dedicated application role. Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup.' ... migrate.sh then loops only over db/migrations/*.sql ... and never invokes db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql ... Net effect: after a reset, the cedar database, schema, tables, and RLS policies all come back, but cedar_app does not.
